cards.back: A quantitative expression of a plan for a specific future period, including planned revenues, costs, cash flows, and capital expenditures.
An integrated set of functional budgets covering the entire organisation (sales, production, materials, labour, overhead, cash, and financial statements).
The starting budget that projects expected unit sales and revenue. All other budgets depend on it.
Calculates units to be produced: Expected Sales + Desired Ending Inventory - Beginning Inventory.
Forecasts cash inflows and outflows to identify periods of surplus or shortfall. Critical for managing working capital and ensuring liquidity.
Budgeting method that adjusts prior period figures up or down. Quick and simple but may perpetuate past inefficiencies.
Budgeting method requiring every expenditure to be justified from scratch. Thorough and eliminates waste but time-consuming.
Continuously updated budget that adds a new period as the prior period expires. Always looks forward (e.g., always 12 months ahead).
Senior management sets revenue and spending targets for departments. Fast but may ignore operational realities.
Department managers provide input on targets. More accurate and increases buy-in but time-consuming.
Comparing actual results against budget to identify and investigate differences. Supports management by exception and accountability.

cards.front: What are favourable and unfavourable variances?
cards.back: Favourable (F): Actual profit > Budgeted profit (good) OR Actual costs < Budgeted costs (good). Unfavourable (U): Opposite (requires investigation).
